case_name: Groundwater recharge treatment selection for pathogen-antibiotic contamination with seasonal flow and energy limits
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for contaminated groundwater intended for groundwater recharge while balancing pathogen control, trace antibiotic reduction, strontium management, seasonal variability, and cost-energy constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A peri-urban recharge project plans to capture shallow groundwater influenced by livestock activity and fertilizer-storage runoff, then infiltrate the treated water into recharge basins to support aquifer recovery. Monitoring shows seasonal wet-weather spikes in E. coli O157 and chlortetracycline, while strontium is persistently elevated from local geochemistry but is not acutely high enough to force the most energy-intensive desalting route. The project owner has limited power availability and a moderate budget, so the preferred option must reliably address pathogen risk and reduce trace organics without committing to a high-pressure membrane system unless the broader trade-off clearly justifies it.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated water should be suitable for groundwater recharge with robust pathogen reduction, meaningful chlortetracycline control, and stable water quality that avoids creating recharge basin or subsurface impacts.
secondary_goals: maintain compliance resilience during seasonal wet-weather spikes
limit energy consumption and avoid unnecessarily complex high-pressure treatment
reduce fouling and clogging risk in recharge basins
manage strontium to a prudent lower level without over-treating the entire mineral matrix

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer avoid concentrate streams and minimize chemically intensive residuals handling; backwashable solids residuals are acceptable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system must tolerate seasonal pathogen and antibiotic spikes and intermittent turbidity increases without large performance swings

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_screening: buffer seasonal water quality swings and remove coarse solids before downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: reduce suspended matter and particle-associated contaminants to stabilize filtration and disinfection
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: remove fine solids and lower turbidity for recharge basin protection and improved UV performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb chlortetracycline and provide polishing of dissolved organic matter
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: provide strong pathogen inactivation without forming a concentrate stream

applicability_rationale: This route directly addresses the highest recharge risks: microbial safety, suspended solids control, and trace antibiotic reduction.
Seasonal groundwater quality variation favors a train with front-end solids conditioning and a robust final disinfection barrier.
GAC is a practical lower-energy approach for chlortetracycline control compared with high-pressure membrane treatment.

Persistent strontium at 3.6 mg/L is a concern but not so extreme that it outweighs the cost and energy penalty of full desalting; for this case, balanced risk reduction is preferred over maximum strontium removal.
The process chain is aligned with reuse-style treatment logic seen in public guidance emphasizing filtration plus disinfection for nonpotable reclaimed water applications.
potential_risks: Strontium removal will be limited, so this option depends on recharge acceptance of residual dissolved minerals.
Chlortetracycline adsorption capacity can decline if DOC rises seasonally, requiring media monitoring and replacement planning.
UV effectiveness depends on maintaining low turbidity and good upstream filter performance.

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_screening: buffer influent variability and remove debris
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: improve particulate removal and protect downstream oxidation and filtration units
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize chlortetracycline and improve biodegradability of oxidation products
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon: polish biodegradable byproducts and remaining organics

applicability_rationale: This is a technically strong option for trace antibiotic control and can outperform GAC-only polishing when seasonal chlortetracycline spikes are significant.
The BAC step helps manage ozonation byproducts and adds organic polishing.
The route remains concentrate-free, which is beneficial for residuals management relative to membrane systems.
It ranks below the top option because energy demand, controls, and O&M complexity are higher, which matters under the stated power and cost constraints.
potential_risks: Ozone systems increase electrical demand and require tighter process control.
Bromate or other oxidation byproduct concerns must be evaluated based on bromide and operating conditions.
Strontium removal remains limited because the main benefit is organics and pathogen control rather than dissolved divalent ion reduction.

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_screening: smooth seasonal flow and solids peaks
core_unit_functions.microfiltration_or_ultrafiltration: provide reliable particulate and bacteria removal and stable low-turbidity effluent
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: reduce chlortetracycline and dissolved organic matter
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: final inactivation barrier for microbial robustness

applicability_rationale: This train gives very strong physical pathogen barrier performance and stable UV transmittance, which is attractive for recharge protection.
Membrane pretreatment can improve consistency during seasonal turbidity upsets better than conventional media filtration alone.
GAC still provides the key lower-energy dissolved organic and antibiotic polishing step.
It ranks behind the top conventional route because membrane pretreatment adds capital cost, maintenance burden, and energy use without solving the strontium issue.
potential_risks: Membrane fouling and backwash management increase O&M demands.
Chlortetracycline removal still depends on GAC replacement frequency.
Dissolved strontium passes through MF and UF, so mineral control remains limited.

core_unit_functions.softening_or_ion_exchange_for_divalent_c: reduce strontium along with hardness-forming divalent ions
core_unit_functions.cartridge_filtration: remove resin fines or precipitate carryover before disinfection

applicability_rationale: This route is included because it directly targets the dissolved strontium concern better than filtration plus GAC alone.
Ion exchange or softening can be a focused strategy when divalent ion reduction is prioritized for recharge compatibility.
It ranks lower because it does not provide as comprehensive and resilient chlortetracycline control as GAC or ozone-BAC and introduces chemical or brine management burdens.
Under the stated project drivers, pathogen and antibiotic control during seasonal spikes have higher practical priority than maximizing strontium removal.
potential_risks: Brine or spent regenerant handling can be difficult for a recharge project with residuals constraints.
If lime softening is used, sludge handling and pH adjustment become significant.
Antibiotic reduction may be inadequate without adding a dedicated adsorption or oxidation polishing step.

core_unit_functions.pretreatment: protect membranes from fouling and scaling
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: remove dissolved strontium and provide strong rejection of chlortetracycline and many other dissolved constituents
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for recharge compatibility

applicability_rationale: RO is technically the most comprehensive option for simultaneous strontium and chlortetracycline removal and can contribute to a very high-quality recharge feed.
It ranks last because the case emphasizes cost and energy sensitivity, and RO creates a concentrate management problem that conflicts with the residuals preference.
This option is over-aligned with maximum dissolved constituent removal and under-aligned with the project's need for practical, lower-energy seasonal operation.
potential_risks: Concentrate disposal is a major challenge and may be unacceptable for the site.
Scaling control, membrane cleaning, and operator skill requirements are substantial.
Post-treatment stabilization is necessary before recharge to avoid overly aggressive water chemistry.

preferred_rank_pattern: filtration_plus_GAC_plus_UV or similar balanced multibarrier routes should rank near the top
an oxidation_plus_biological_polishing route should appear as a strong but more energy-intensive alternative
RO should not rank first under high energy sensitivity and residuals constraints
a strontium-targeted softening_or_ion_exchange option should appear but rank below more balanced pathogen-and-antibiotic focused routes

must_reflect: seasonal pathogen and antibiotic spikes require robust multibarrier treatment
energy sensitivity should penalize ozone-heavy or high-pressure membrane options
cost limitations should favor conventional filtration and adsorption over full desalting
residuals and concentrate handling should materially affect ranking

must_contain: why filtration plus disinfection is relevant for groundwater recharge or reuse-style treatment
why GAC or oxidation-polishing is appropriate for chlortetracycline reduction
why strontium can justify ion-exchange or RO technically but not necessarily as the top-ranked choice
why seasonal variability changes the value of equalization and robust solids control

should_mention: GAC breakthrough or reduced adsorption capacity during DOC increases
insufficient strontium reduction in lower-energy options
